editableNewDialogue.text: hey, quick question—what’s the difference between a webhook and polling in plain terms?

Polling is when your system repeatedly asks another system “anything changed yet?” on a schedule. A webhook is when the other system proactively sends your system a message the moment something changes. Webhooks usually reduce wasted requests and feel more real-time, while polling can be simpler but may be slower or more resource-heavy.
if webhooks are real-time, why would anyone still use polling?

A few common reasons: some services don’t offer webhooks; firewalls/network rules can block inbound callbacks; webhook delivery can fail and needs retries/signature validation; and sometimes the “change event” isn’t detailed enough, so you still need to poll for the full data. Many systems use a hybrid: webhook triggers, then a follow-up fetch to get details.
does a webhook always include the full data payload?

Not always. Some webhooks send full resource details; others only send an ID, timestamp, or a pointer to what changed. In those cases, your app receives the event and then calls the API to retrieve the updated item(s).
One small thing I forgot to mention: I’m trying to keep the whole report consistent in British English, because some parts still slip into American spelling.

Good call—pick one variant and apply it everywhere. For British English consistency, do a quick sweep for common US/UK differences (e.g., “organize”→“organise”, “behavior”→“behaviour”, “center”→“centre”), and also check punctuation conventions (e.g., single quotes for terms if that’s your chosen style). If you want, paste a paragraph and I’ll mark the spots that break the chosen variant.
Small correction: this is not my internship at companyX. I’m writing feedback on someone else’s report. I don’t work there and I’m not developing that sync tool myself.

Thanks for clarifying—so you’re reviewing a report written by an intern at companyX, not describing your own internship. That means references like “during my internship” and “my sync tool” are the author’s perspective, and your role is to assess whether their write-up matches the required structure and is clear/complete.
